---
title: "American Bluehearts (Buchnera americana)"
slug: "buchnera-americana"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476246811/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "American Bluehearts"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Lamiales"
tax_family: "Orobanchaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Lamiales", "Orobanchaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/476246811/medium.jpg" alt="American Bluehearts" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/garccyy">
garccyy</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/265164742">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Lamiales
-   **Family:** Orobanchaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=126699)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Uncommon/Rare species in Florida found in moist to wet sandy soils.

## Natural history notes

A slender wildflower characterized by its narrow leaves and small blue to violet tubular flowers borne along upright stems. In Florida, it is found in open, sandy habitats such as flatwoods, savannas, and prairies. It typically blooms during the warmer months and, as a hemiparasitic plant, obtains some nutrients from the roots of nearby grasses and other plants.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/126699-Buchnera-americana)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-buchnera-americana/)
